posts.name: Alex North
posts.summary: A single post-purchase 'How did you hear about us?' survey question captures attribution data that no pixel or cookie can match, including dark social and word-of-mouth channels. Brands combining this with order data get real CAC and LTV by source, revealing revenue from channels like podcasts and referrals they never knew they had.
posts.a: The 'channels they can't track digitally' part hits. Dark social and podcast referrals are massive blind spots for most brands. Asking customers directly is genuinely the most reliable signal. 🙌
posts.b: Honestly this is what we tell anyone chasing better attribution: start with the source question before investing in expensive tooling. The customer usually knows exactly how they found you.

posts.name: Priya Verma, MBA
posts.role: Fractional CS Consultant, helping Series A/B SaaS founders fix broken Customer Success systems
posts.summary: Took a Series B SaaS from 18% monthly churn to 7% in 90 days without hiring a single new CSM. Key moves: built a health scoring system using 12 leading indicators, segmented the book into 3 tiers, automated low-touch communication to give CSMs 15 hours back per week, then launched a value-based expansion motion. NRR climbed from 94% to 112%.
posts.a: Segmenting into 3 tiers is the move that usually unlocks everything else. Most teams treat every customer the same until they literally can't. The 94% to 112% NRR jump from that shift is huge. 🙌
posts.b: The '15 hours back per week' from automating low-touch comms is what makes the expansion motion possible. Hard to run strategic QBRs when you're buried in 1:1 firefighting.

posts.name: Elegant Joy Okah-Tim
posts.role: Customer Success Manager | Technical Account Manager
posts.summary: 5 early churn signals every CSM should monitor: declining logins, loss of internal champion, shrinking team engagement, unanswered check-ins, and scope creep complaints. Each is a quiet whisper that compounds into a goodbye if you ignore enough of them. The best CSMs are early warning systems, not firefighters.
posts.a: Loss of internal champion is the sneaky one. You can have great login data and usage metrics and still be fully exposed if the person who bought into your product walks out the door. 🙏
posts.b: This made me think about how often unanswered check-ins get logged and moved on from. That's actually the moment to escalate, not just retry the same email.

posts.name: Webtopia
posts.summary: YouTube delivered 86% higher incremental long-term ROAS than paid social across 10 US CPG brands and 20 campaigns over 24 months. 45% of YouTube Shorts users aren't on TikTok and 65% aren't on Instagram Reels. 40% of YouTube views happen 30+ days after posting. The content compounds in ways Meta ads simply don't.
posts.a: The 'content compounds' framing is what makes this math work. Meta spend stops the moment your billing stops. YouTube keeps running. The 86% gap makes a lot more sense when you think about shelf life. 🙌
posts.b: Honestly the 45% of Shorts users who aren't on TikTok is the sleeper number here. For DTC brands that feel like they've maxed their Meta audience, that's a real untapped pool.

posts.name: Ramesh Subramaniam
posts.role: Co-founder, Yuko Loyalty & Retainful | Helping 20,000+ DTC brands grow through retention
posts.summary: Wellbeing Nutrition's cart shows 3 progressive free gift unlocks at different spend thresholds, making savings visible before checkout. Most carts just show product, price, and a checkout button. Making the goal visible while you're still shopping feels like the brand is actually thinking about the experience.
posts.a: Making savings visible before checkout is doing so much work here. Most brands wait until the upsell popup to reveal value and by then you're already interrupting momentum. 🙌
posts.b: The 'savings sitting right there in the cart' detail is what makes this stick. It's not a discount, it's a goal the customer is already most of the way to hitting.
